what_it_does: Defines an ITEM_TYPE registration for a ledger item called 'signal', describing it as a shared-tree resume notification that a lane consumes to act, with an initial state of 'raised' and a finite state machine over raised/consumed/superseded.
observations: The file contains a single ITEM_TYPE dictionary with keys id, label, desc, initial, states, and transitions.
The description frames the signal as the 'shared-tree, floor-clean half of the operator cycle's resume wire' and links it to a decision:// address.
Transitions allow raised -> consumed or superseded; consumed -> superseded; superseded -> raised.
The signal is described as an 'it changed' relation rather than the carrier of the decided value.
It distinguishes the shared-tree signal from a separate gated live-MCP channel post.
standouts: The superseded -> raised transition lets a later re-decide re-raise the signal, mirroring a re-decide cycle.
The signal is intentionally a relation/notification, not the storage location for the decision value.
It explicitly contrasts cross-session shared-tree visibility with an autonomous-spawn-lead-only live-MCP channel post.
conventions: Follows the ITEM_TYPE registration pattern with id, label, desc, initial, states, and transitions.
Uses a multi-line concatenated string for a long description.
Models lifecycle as a small explicit finite state machine.
concerns: The description is dense with domain jargon and may be hard to interpret without external context.
No validation metadata or schema is provided for the decision:// address or attached_to relation.
There are no code symbols, so behavior is entirely implied by the state machine and prose.
notes: This file registers the 'signal' ledger item type and its lifecycle, treating the signal as a shared-tree resume marker attached to a decision address.
questions: What exactly is a 'lane' in this system?
What is the 'gated live-MCP channel post' and how does it differ mechanically from the shared-tree signal?
Who or what posts a signal, and who consumes it?
How is the attached_to decision:// address enforced or represented in the item?
What triggers a transition to 'superseded' versus 'consumed' in practice?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the content: it defines a signal item type that lanes consume to resume gated work, with states and transitions reflecting decide/consume/supersede cycles.
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to model a lightweight, shared-tree 'something changed' notification that lets downstream work resume when a decision is decided, while keeping the actual decision value and live notifications separate.
novelty: The signal is deliberately a change-relation rather than a value container, and its state machine includes a superseded-to-raised loop to support repeated re-decides.
contribution: Provides the 'signal' item type definition and lifecycle states/transitions that a fused system would keep if it needs a shared-tree resume marker tied to decisions.
summary_for_embedding: This file registers the 'signal' ledger item type, an 'it changed' resume marker consumed by lanes, with states raised/consumed/superseded and transitions supporting re-raise after re-decide.
intention_for_embedding: This file was intended to define the lifecycle of a shared-tree signal that notifies lanes when a decision has been decided so gated work can resume.
